leaky.description: Run with data leakage: prompt told agent the visible trend would reverse
leaky.analysis: Direction accuracy of 86% is dramatically above the 33% random baseline for a three-class (rising/falling/stable) problem, confirming the model successfully uses cross-lab signals to detect reversals rather than naively following the visible troponin trend. However, the benefit is almost entirely confined to direction: only 17% of cases land within 50% of the true value and just 10% within 20%, producing a mean score of 0.43 out of 1.0 — dominated by the 0.40 floor that a correct direction alone earns. The sharpest asymmetry is across reversal type: falling→rising cases achieve 100% direction accuracy (35/35) but only 3% within-50% accuracy, meaning the model consistently identifies that a rebound is coming while dramatically underestimating its magnitude — likely because it anchors on the last visible low value (often 0.01–0.4 ng/mL) and fails to project the true acute-event scale (often 3–40 ng/mL). The model shows a strong rising bias in its predictions: 61 of 100 predicted directions were "rising" against only 50 actual rising cases, and 10 of the 14 direction failures were cases where the true direction was falling but the model predicted rising — concentrated almost entirely in stable→falling cases (9 of 15 failed), the hardest category because the visible plateau provides no momentum cue and cross-lab falling signals (normalizing BNP, improving renal function) are subtler than acute-injury signals. The best magnitude accuracy appears in rising→falling cases (34% within 50%, 20% within 20%), which is plausible: after a clear visible rise the model has a concrete peak value to anchor on and can estimate the post-peak clearance trajectory using troponin kinetics, whereas for falling→rising and stable→rising cases it must estimate the size of a future event from indirect signals alone.

debiased.description: Run after removing data leakage from prompt and question stem
debiased.analysis: Overall direction accuracy is 54% (54/100), substantially above the 33% random baseline for a three-way classification (rising/falling/stable), confirming that the model extracts meaningful signal from cross-lab patterns even when the troponin trend itself is designed to mislead. The accuracy is strongly stratified by reversal type: stable→rising (80%, 12/15) and stable→falling (73%, 11/15) cases are easiest because a flat baseline gives the model no conflicting momentum to override, whereas falling→rising (63%, 22/35) is moderately hard and rising→falling (26%, 9/35) is the clear weak point — barely above chance. The model exhibits pronounced rising bias on rising→falling cases: of the 26 incorrect predictions in that group, 19 predicted "rising" (continuing the visible trend), 4 predicted "stable", and only 3 missed by predicting the wrong non-falling direction, showing that the model struggles most to call a peak-and-reversal when the last observed value is still climbing. Magnitude estimation is uniformly poor: only 7% of cases land within 50% of the actual value and only 3% within 20%, indicating that while the model can often detect the direction of reversal it severely underestimates the magnitude of the next value — particularly for falling→rising cases where the predicted value is frequently anchored near the near-normal last troponin rather than reflecting the large secondary rise signaled by CK, BNP, or hemodynamic deterioration. The cases with perfect scores (39, 72, 84) all share a pattern of gradual, well-telegraphed falling trajectories with small gap windows and low actual values, suggesting that cross-lab signals are most actionable when the magnitude of change is modest and the clinical context is unambiguous. Improving performance on rising→falling reversals likely requires the model to weight CK-MB decline, LDH plateau, and time-since-peak more aggressively relative to the last troponin delta, as the current reasoning summaries show over-reliance on the steepness of the visible rising phase.
